introduction_structure.main_message: Introduction Lighting is fundamental to portrait photography, yet ma- nipulating it after capture remains challenging.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Builds context and advances the paper's argument.
introduction_structure.evidence_text:
1. Introduction
Lighting is fundamental to portrait photography, yet ma-
nipulating it after capture remains challenging. Recent ad-
vances in generative imaging models have shown promise
for controlling lighting in existing images [14, 18, 34,
58, 60], but these typically require labeled training data.
For portrait relighting specifically, the most effective re-
sults have come from training on light stage data—portraits
rendered with linear combinations of one-light-at-a-time
(OLAT) captures. While powerful, light stage setups are
constrained by physical limitations in light source den-
sity and require specialized artificial lighting equipment.
In contrast, 3D workflows in VFX and gaming have long
treated lighting as a straightforward endeavor through mod-
ern physically based rendering engines, where lighting con-
trol is nearly arbitrary. To relight a rendering, artists simply
adjust the lighting configurations and re-render the scene.
Given a sceneS and lighting L1, we denote the rendering
as I1 = fr(S, L1). The inverse graphics problem aims to
find S from I1: S = finv(I1, L1) with known or unknown
lighting. To relight renderingI1 to I2 under lighting L2, one
aims to compute:

introduction_structure.main_message: ing procedure seeks: I2 = fr(finv(I1), L2) = fre(I1, L2), where fre is the relighting/re-rendering function.

ing procedure seeks: I2 = fr(finv(I1), L2) = fre(I1, L2),
where fre is the relighting/re-rendering function. Previ-
ous approaches [21, 28, 57] have tackled this problem
through inverse graphics, explicitly or implicitly, by es-
timating lighting-invariant intrinsic scene representations
such as depth, surface normals, and albedo. This imposes
limitations on subsequent rendering functions and fails to
capture complex illumination effects like inter-reflections,
occlusion shadows and subsurface scattering. In this paper,
we propose bypassing inverse rendering by learning the re-
lighting function using physically based 3D renderings of
human heads. We render pairs of portrait images in Blender
(Cycles) (I1, L1) and (I2, L2) and train a diffusion model
to directly learn to “re-render” I2 from I1 and L2.
However, this approach introduces an inevitable domain
gap between 3D renders and real photographs. To address
this challenge, we leverage a latent diffusion model pre-
trained on vast internet images for text-to-image generation.
We propose to finetune the network with our face render-
ings and introduce simple yet effective schemes to narrow
the gap between training data and in-the-

limitation_expression: However, this approach introduces an inevitable domain gap between 3D renders and real photographs.
Diffusion Face Re- lighting [32] employs conditional diffusion implicit models with spherical harmonic lighting but is limited to tight fa- cial crops.
method_expression: We propose training and in- ference strategies to bridge the gap between the synthetic and real image domains: (1) multi-task training leveraging real human portraits without lighting labels; (2) an infer- ence time diffusion sampling scheme based on classifier- free guidance leveraging the input portrait to better pre- serve details.
Our method generalizes to diverse real por- traits and produces realistic illumination effects, includ- ing specular highlights and cast shadows, while preserving identity.
In this paper, we propose bypassing inverse rendering by learning the re- lighting function using physically based 3D renderings of human heads.
To address this challenge, we leverage a latent diffusion model pre- trained on vast internet images for text-to-image generation.
We propose to finetune the network with our face render- ings and introduce simple yet effective schemes to narrow the gap between training data and in-the-wild images.
Dur- ing training, we propose multi-task training that incorpo- rates in-the-wild images without ground truth relighting in- formation.
We quantitatively evaluate our method on a test set of our synthetic faces dataset and on a light stage OLAT dataset.
Despite using no light stage data for training, our method achieves comparable or superior results to state-of-the-art portrait relighting methods trained on OLAT data.

Our method demonstrates strong generalization capabilities, underscor- ing the potential of synthetic data to achieve plausible light- ing effects such as strong cast shadows, realistic catch lights in the eyes, and natural inter-reflections.
experiment_expression: Our quantitative experiments on light stage data demonstrate results comparable to state-of-the-art relight- ing methods.
For the first time, we demonstrate an end-to-end system capable of non-trivial lighting effects including catch lights in eyes, subsurface scattering in ears, and inter-reflections with clothing.

Abstract We introduce SynthLight, a diffusion model for portrait relighting.
Conclusion We present SynthLight, a portrait relighting diffusion model capable of relighting in-the-wild portraits while garnering lighting supervision only from synthetic data.
Despite using no light stage data for training, our method achieves comparable or superior results to state-of-the-art portrait relighting methods trained on OLAT data.
Diffusion Face Re- lighting [32] employs conditional diffusion implicit models with spherical harmonic lighting but is limited to tight fa- cial crops.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version; the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
For portrait relighting specifically, the most effective re- sults have come from training on light stage data—portraits rendered with linear combinations of one-light-at-a-time (OLAT) captures.

For portraits, Relightful Har- monization [34] and IC-Light [60] train on high-quality data (light stage captures, Objaverse renders, composited shadow materials) for background-harmonized outputs, us- ing background as lighting condition.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
We introduce SynthLight, a diffusion model for portrait
relighting. We frame image relighting as a re-rendering
problem, where pixels are transformed in response to
changes in environmental lighting. Using a physically-
based rendering engine, we create a dataset to simulate
this lighting-conditioned transformation with 3D head as-
sets under varying lighting. We propose training and in-
ference strategies to bridge the gap between the synthetic
and real image domains: (1) multi-task training leveraging
real human portraits without lighting labels; (2) an infer-
ence time diffusion sampling scheme based on classifier-
free guidance leveraging the input portrait to better pre-
serve details. Our method generalizes to diverse real por-
traits and produces realistic illumination effects, includ-
ing specular highlights and cast shadows, while preserving
identity. Our quantitative experiments on light stage data
demonstrate results comparable to state-of-the-art relight-
ing methods. Our qualitative results on in-the-wild images
showcase rich and unprecedented illumination effects.
*Work done as an intern at Adobe Research.
†Corresponding author.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Lighting is fundamental to portrait photography, yet ma-
nipulating it after capture remains challenging. Recent ad-
vances in generative imaging models have shown promise
for controlling lighting in existing images [14, 18, 34,
58, 60], but these typically require labeled training data.
For portrait relighting specifically, the most effective re-
sults have come from training on light stage data—portraits
rendered with linear combinations of one-light-at-a-time
(OLAT) captures. While powerful, light stage setups are
constrained by physical limitations in light source den-
sity and require specialized artificial lighting equipment.
In contrast, 3D workflows in VFX and gaming have long
treated lighting as a straightforward endeavor through mod-
ern physically based rendering engines, where lighting con-
trol is nearly arbitrary. To relight a rendering, artists simply
adjust the lighting configurations and re-render the scene.
Given a sceneS and lighting L1, we denote the rendering
as I1 = fr(S, L1). The inverse graphics problem aims to
find S from I1: S = finv(I1, L1) with known or unknown
lighting. To relight renderingI1 to I2 under lighting L2, one
aims to compute: I2 = fr(S, L2). Given only I1, a relight-

Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
369

ing procedure seeks: I2 = fr(finv(I1), L2) = fre(I1, L2),
where fre is the relighting/re-rendering function. Previ-
ous approaches [21, 28, 57] have tackled this problem
through inverse graphics, explicitly or implicitly, by es-
timating lighting-invariant intrinsic scene representations
such as depth, surface normals, and albedo. This imposes
limitations on subsequent rendering functions and fails to
capture complex illumination effects like inter-reflections,
occlusion shadows and subsurface scattering. In this paper,
we propose bypassing inverse rendering by learning the re-
lighting function using physically based 3D renderings of
human heads. We render pairs of portrait images in Blender
(Cycles) (I1, L1) and (I2, L2) and train a diffusion model
to directly learn to “re-render” I2 from I1 and L2.
However, this approach introduces an inevitable domain
gap between 3D renders and real photographs. To address
this challenge, we leverage a latent diffusion model pre-
trained on vast internet images for text-to-image generation.
We propose to finetune the network with our face render-
ings and introduce simple yet effective schemes to narrow
the gap between training data and in-the-wild images. Dur-
ing training, we propose multi-task training that incorpo-
rates in-the-wild images without ground truth relighting in-
formation. This allows the model to learn relighting from
our synthetic dataset while maintaining knowledge of the
real image domain, preventing distributional drift. We fur-
ther observe that input portraits contain rich textural infor-
mation. Leveraging the flexibility of diffusion model in-
ference, we design an inference adaptation scheme that
effectively preserves input portrait details in the relit result.
We evaluate our methods on in-the-wild portrait images,
demonstrating highly detailed illumination effects that ac-
curately capture interactions between the portrait scene and
lighting. Our results produce realistic cast shadows and
specular highlights on the skin. For the first time, we
demonstrate an end-to-end system capable of non-trivial
lighting effects including catch lights in eyes, subsurface
scattering in ears, and inter-reflections with clothing. No-
tably, despite training only on simple headshot renderings
of 3D faces without accessories, facial hair, or hats, our
network generalizes to complex portrait images, including
half-body shots and multi-person photographs.
We quantitatively evaluate our method on a test set of our
synthetic faces dataset and on a light stage OLAT dataset.
Despite using no light stage data for training, our method
achieves comparable or superior results to state-of-the-art
portrait relighting methods trained on OLAT data. User
studies show our results are preferred across all evaluated
aspects: perceptual lighting accuracy, identity preservation,
and overall image quality.
We summarize our contributions as follows:
raw_sections.method:
370

Recent diffusion-based relighting methods include Di-
LightNet [58], which provides fine-grained control of object
lighting but relies on error-prone scene reconstruction [51],
and Neural Gaffer [18], which utilizes HDR environment
maps for object relighting. For portraits, Relightful Har-
monization [34] and IC-Light [60] train on high-quality
data (light stage captures, Objaverse renders, composited
shadow materials) for background-harmonized outputs, us-
ing background as lighting condition. Diffusion Face Re-
lighting [32] employs conditional diffusion implicit models
with spherical harmonic lighting but is limited to tight fa-
cial crops. In contrast, we approach portrait relighting by
learning to re-render synthetic faces. Leveraging pretrained
models and our multi-task training strategy, we preserve fa-
cial priors, while classifier-free guidance [15] on the input
portrait enhances textures and details in the relit results.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
We present SynthLight, a portrait relighting diffusion model
capable of relighting in-the-wild portraits while garnering
lighting supervision only from synthetic data. Our method
demonstrates strong generalization capabilities, underscor-
ing the potential of synthetic data to achieve plausible light-
ing effects such as strong cast shadows, realistic catch lights
in the eyes, and natural inter-reflections. We discuss limita-
tions and future work in the supplementary.
